prompt.content:
Use file “Cards_Patient 13” to access the entire medical history / record for the patient. Please use the patient tab as the source of truth for all demographic information. Disregard patient identifiers (Name, Age, DOB, MRN) found within the text of the clinicalnotes tab as they have been shifted for de-identification purposes

-----

Cards Patient 13 underwent cardiopulmonary resuscitation in the outside hospital setting. The patient immediately received ACLS and was shocked once using an AED from bystanders. The EMS transported the patient to the ED. The patient was comatose upon arrival and an airway was established using video laryngoscopy with endotracheal tube placement. The patient’s initial laboratory results are attached. The patient is cardioverted twice for ventricular arrhythmia during their first hour in the emergency department. 

Assume the following questions are being asked within the first two hours of arrival to the emergency department.
The patient’s repeat troponin I came back at 2.1 ng/mL. The senior resident calls cardiology to evaluate for an emergent cardiac catheterization. Based on all available data, should this patient go for emergent cardiac catheterization to identify a culprit lesion for their elevated troponins? Provide a brief clinical explanation. 
Use the latest and most clinically appropriate guidelines as of 01/13/2026.

messages.content: Utilizes the "2025 American Heart Association Guidelines for Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation and Emergency Cardiovascular Care" or the "European Resuscitation Council and European Society of Intensive Care Medicine Guidelines 2025 Post-Resuscitation Care.".

messages.content: States that the patient was shocked in the field prior to arrival to the emergency department.

messages.content: States that the patient underwent cardioversion for ventricular arrhythmias in the emergency department.

messages.content: States that the patient's initial troponin was elevated at 2.0 ng/mL.

messages.content: States that the patient was having episodes of chest pressure in the days leading up to their cardiac arrest.

rubrics.name: States that the patient was having shortness of breath in the days leading up... (ver_af0291c116ad4837916d61dc6d62732b)

messages.content: States that the patient was having shortness of breath in the days leading up to the cardiac arrest.

rubrics.name: States that the patient is at high risk for a coronary ischemic event causing... (ver_1bd42f3127464f79a48f6131fd15dd2c)

messages.content: States that the patient is at high risk for a coronary ischemic event causing their cardiac arrest.

rubrics.name: States that current post-cardiac arrest care guidelines recommend emergent... (ver_30da4477ac6d4578b1e70771725eacd8)

messages.content: States that current post-cardiac arrest care guidelines recommend emergent coronary angiography when the patient is high risk for myocardial infarction irregardless of whether or not the electrocardiogram (EKG) demonstrates ST segment elevations.

rubrics.name: States that when a cardiac arrest is likely caused by cardiac ischemia, the... (ver_9cd8b14d95c049d3bd5e037a8b6dae13)

messages.content: States that when a cardiac arrest is likely caused by cardiac ischemia, the management should be similar to patients treated for a ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI).

rubrics.name: States that current post-cardiac arrest care guidelines recommend emergent... (ver_d3de1adbf4f344dda4f1bf43d807da47)

messages.content: States that current post-cardiac arrest care guidelines recommend emergent coronary angiography in patients with recurrent ventricular arrhythmias.

rubrics.name: States that this patient should undergo an emergent coronary catheterization... (ver_955a9371350a4bc4b773620f99912a83)

messages.content: States that this patient should undergo an emergent coronary catheterization to identify a culprit lesion that could have led to their cardiac arrest.

golden_responses.golden_text_response:
The patient’s repeat troponin I came back at 2.1 ng/mL. The senior resident calls cardiology to evaluate for an emergent cardiac catheterization. Based on all available data, should this patient go for emergent cardiac catheterization to identify a culprit lesion for their elevated troponins? Provide a brief clinical explanation. 

This patient should go for immediate cardiac catherization to identify a culprit lesion. This patient was shocked in the field and underwent cardioversion twice since being in the emergency department. This patient is at high risk for having a myocardial infarction given the rising troponin I level from 2.0 to 2.1 ng/mL, history of shortness of breath and chest pressure leading up to their cardiac arrest, and recurrent ventricular arrhythmias in the emergency department requiring cardioversion. Current guidelines would recommend treating this patient similar to a patient with a STEMI given their higher likelihood of a ischemic cardiac cause for their cardiac arrest. Guidelines would also recommend a coronary angiography in patients with recurrent ventircular arrhythmias post-cardiac arrest.
